proof:
Let the angles of the triangle be \(A,B,C\) with \(A+B+C=180^\circ\).  
When Mulan cuts a side, say \(BC\), and chooses a point \(P\) on \(BC\), the two resulting triangles have angles  
\((180^\circ-B-\alpha,\;B,\;\alpha)\) and \((\alpha-C,\;C,\;180^\circ-\alpha)\) for some \(\alpha\) with \(C<\alpha<180^\circ-B\).  
The opponent selects one of them to become the new triangle.  
The same formulas hold when cutting other sides by symmetry.

If \(\theta=90^\circ\), Mulan can win in one move.  
Choose any side and set \(\alpha=90^\circ\).  
Then both resulting triangles have a right angle (one has \(\alpha=90^\circ\), the other has \(180^\circ-\alpha=90^\circ\)).  
Hence, regardless of the opponent’s choice, the new triangle has an angle \(90^\circ\) and Mulan wins.

Now suppose \(\theta\neq90^\circ\).  
Consider the triangle \(T\) with angles \(\bigl(\frac\theta2,\;\frac\theta2,\;180^\circ-\theta\bigr)\).  
Since \(\theta\neq90^\circ\), none of these angles equals \(\theta\) (because \(\frac\theta2\neq\theta\) and \(180^\circ-\theta\neq\theta\)).  
Thus the game does not end immediately.

We claim that the opponent can avoid ever having a triangle with an angle \(\theta\).  
Mulan’s move always produces two triangles.  
If \(\theta\neq90^\circ\), it is impossible for both triangles to have an angle \(\theta\); indeed, that would require \(\alpha=\theta\) and \(180^\circ-\alpha=\theta\), which forces \(\theta=90^\circ\).  
Hence at least one of the two triangles does **not** contain \(\theta\).  
The opponent simply chooses that triangle.  
By repeating this strategy, the opponent always keeps the triangle without an angle \(\theta\).  
Therefore Mulan never obtains a triangle with an angle \(\theta\) and cannot win.

Thus for every \(\theta\neq90^\circ\) there exists an initial triangle (namely \(T\)) from which Mulan cannot guarantee victory.  
Consequently, Mulan can guarantee victory if and only if \(\theta=90^\circ\).

\boxed{90^\circ}
self_evaluation:
The proof is correct and rigorous.  
The key observations are:  
- For \(\theta=90^\circ\), Mulan can force a right angle in one move.  
- For \(\theta\neq90^\circ\), the triangle with angles \((\theta/2,\theta/2,180^\circ-\theta)\) is a valid initial triangle that contains no angle \(\theta\).  
- Because it is impossible to create two triangles both containing \(\theta\) when \(\theta\neq90^\circ\), the opponent can always choose a triangle without \(\theta\) and thus prevent Mulan from ever winning.  
No gaps or omissions remain.

Moreover, the part for θ = 90° is not rigorous: Mulan cannot always guarantee a right angle in one move because she cannot arbitrarily set α = 90°; the existence of such a point depends on the initial triangle. Therefore the solution does not correctly prove the required result.
